cards.front: What is a syntax error in C++?
cards.back: An error that occurs when you write a statement that is not valid according to the grammar of the C++ language (e.g., missing semicolons, mismatched braces, invalid identifiers).

cards.front: What is a semantic error in C++?
cards.back: An error in meaning where the code is syntactically valid but either violates language rules or doesn't do what the programmer intended.

cards.text: C++ errors fall into two main categories: {{c1::syntax errors}} and {{c2::semantic errors}} (also called logic errors).

cards.back: At compile-time. The compiler will detect syntax errors and issue compilation warnings or errors.

cards.question: Identify THREE syntax errors in this code:
cards.answer:
• Missing closing parenthesis after main(
  • Variable name 1x starts with a number (invalid identifier)
  • Missing semicolon after return 0
cards.explanation: Also note the extraneous semicolon after the string literal, though this is a less critical error.

cards.front: What are two types of semantic errors based on when they manifest?
cards.back:
• Compile-time semantic errors (detected by compiler)
  • Runtime semantic errors (manifest during program execution)

cards.text: Examples of compile-time semantic errors include using an {{c1::undeclared variable}} and {{c2::type mismatches}}.

cards.question: What are the semantic errors in this code?
cards.answer:
• Variable x is not declared
  • Cannot assign a value to literal 5 (not an lvalue)
  • Cannot return string literal from function returning int
cards.explanation: These are all compile-time semantic errors that the compiler will catch.

cards.question: What runtime error will this code cause?
cards.answer: Division by zero - this is undefined behavior in mathematics and C++.
cards.explanation: Division by zero will likely crash the program or produce undefined results.

cards.question: What semantic error is present in this code?
cards.answer: Use of uninitialized variable. Variable x has no initializer, so it contains a garbage value (indeterminate value).
cards.explanation: This leads to undefined behavior. The output will be unpredictable.

cards.question: What will this code output and why is it wrong?
cards.answer:
Output: "5 + 3 = 2"

The function is supposed to add but uses subtraction operator instead (wrong operator used in implementation).
cards.explanation: This is a logic error where the function doesn't match its intended behavior.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: The cout statement is unreachable code - it appears after the return statement so it will never execute.
cards.explanation: This is a logic error. The function returns before the output statement can run.

cards.text: {{c1::Syntax errors}} are caught at {{c2::compile-time}} by the compiler, while runtime {{c3::semantic errors}} only appear when the program executes.
Common syntax errors include missing {{c1::semicolons}}, mismatched {{c2::braces}}, and invalid {{c3::identifiers}}.
